description: The 'Garden of France' stretches 1,000km along the Loire River, producing benchmark Sauvignon Blanc, ethereal Chenin Blanc and age-worthy Cabernet Franc.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Roman viticulture along the Loire dates to the 1st century CE.

appellations.name: Sancerre
appellations.description: Perched on Kimmeridgian limestone above the Loire, Sancerre is the world's most precise Sauvignon Blanc — flinty, mineral, aristocratic. Its silex soils impart an unmistakable gunflint character.
appellations.climate: Cool continental — cold winters, warm summers, spring frost risk.

tastingProfile.style: Flinty, mineral, aristocratic Sauvignon Blanc

appellations.name: Pouilly-Fumé
appellations.description: Directly across the Loire from Sancerre, grown exclusively on silex soils. The 'fumé' refers to the smoky, gunpowder character the flint imparts. Richer and rounder than Sancerre.
appellations.climate: Cool continental with Loire moderation.

tastingProfile.style: Smoky, flinty, richer-textured than Sancerre

appellations.name: Vouvray
appellations.description: Vouvray produces Chenin Blanc in every style from bone-dry to richly sweet. Dry Vouvrays age 50+ years, developing extraordinary honeyed complexity in tufa limestone caves.
appellations.climate: Oceanic — mild, maritime-influenced.

tastingProfile.style: Electric Chenin Blanc — dry to luscious, ages magnificently

appellations.name: Chinon
appellations.description: The Loire's greatest red appellation. Rabelais called it 'the divine bottle'. Cabernet Franc on gravel and tufa soils produces wines of elegant pencil-lead and raspberry complexity.
appellations.climate: Oceanic — cool, mild; Cabernet Franc ripens slowly and perfectly.

tastingProfile.style: Loire's finest red — elegant, pencil-lead Cabernet Franc

appellations.name: Muscadet Sèvre-et-Maine
appellations.description: The Loire's Atlantic gateway. Muscadet sur lie aged on lees produces dry, saline whites of surprising depth. The best — aged 24–36 months — rival fine Chablis in mineral intensity.
appellations.climate: Atlantic maritime — mild, wet, strongly ocean-influenced.

tastingProfile.style: Bone-dry, saline, lees-aged Atlantic white

appellations.name: Savennières
appellations.description: One of France's most individual white appellations. Savennières produces bone-dry Chenin Blanc of extraordinary mineral austerity from ancient schist and volcanic soils. Coulée de Serrant is often cited as France's greatest dry white.
appellations.climate: Oceanic — warm south-facing schist slopes on the Loire.

tastingProfile.style: Austere, mineral, meditative Chenin Blanc — requires patience

description: The undisputed capital of fine wine. Over 7,000 châteaux, a millennium of winemaking, and the world's most influential classification system (1855).

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Roman amphorae bearing Bacchus imagery found across the Gironde.

appellations.name: Pauillac
appellations.description: Home to three of Bordeaux's five Premier Crus — Lafite, Latour and Mouton Rothschild. Deep gravel terraces produce Cabernet of unrivalled structure, power and longevity.
appellations.climate: Maritime — Atlantic moderation, Gironde estuary influence.

tastingProfile.style: Supremely structured — the most powerful of the Médoc

appellations.name: Margaux
appellations.description: The most perfumed and feminine of the Médoc. Fine gravel soils produce wines of extraordinary floral elegance — violets and cassis intertwined with silky tannins.
appellations.climate: Maritime with excellent drainage on gravel terraces.

tastingProfile.style: The most feminine of the Médoc — perfumed, silky, aristocratic

appellations.name: Saint-Émilion
appellations.description: The right bank jewel of Bordeaux. Merlot dominates on limestone plateau and clay slopes, producing wines of sumptuous texture. The UNESCO-listed medieval town is one of France's most beautiful.
appellations.climate: Maritime, slightly warmer than Médoc; clay retains moisture.

tastingProfile.style: Plush, Merlot-driven right bank opulence

appellations.name: Sauternes
appellations.description: The world's greatest sweet wine appellation. Morning mists from the Ciron river create perfect conditions for noble rot, concentrating grapes to nectar. Château d'Yquem is wine's most celebrated sweet estate.
appellations.climate: Maritime with Ciron river morning mists — ideal botrytis conditions.

tastingProfile.style: Liquid gold — the world's most complex sweet wine

appellations.name: Pomerol
appellations.description: Pomerol is Bordeaux's smallest and most exclusive appellation — no official classification, yet Pétrus commands the world's highest prices. Its iron-rich clay produces Merlot of voluptuous, velvety richness.
appellations.climate: Maritime. Clay soils retain moisture and warmth for Merlot's late ripening.

tastingProfile.style: Bordeaux's most opulent — silky power on iron-rich clay

appellations.name: Pessac-Léognan
appellations.description: Bordeaux's oldest appellation and the only one classifying both reds and whites. Haut-Brion — the sole non-Médoc property in the 1855 Classification — produces wines of haunting tobacco and earth complexity.
appellations.climate: Maritime, with urban heat island effect around Pessac.

tastingProfile.style: Earthy, tobacco-driven — most terroir-expressive of Bordeaux

description: Where terroir is religion. Burgundy's fragmented plots produce the world's most coveted Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. The Côte d'Or — a 50km limestone ridge — is wine's holiest ground.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Autun, Roman capital of Burgundy, hosted one of Gaul's largest Bacchus cults.

appellations.name: Gevrey-Chambertin
appellations.description: Napoleon's favourite wine. Contains 9 Grand Crus including supreme Chambertin. The most powerful and structured wines of the Côte de Nuits, combining dark fruit with iron-fisted tannins.
appellations.climate: Continental — cold winters, warm summers, east-facing slopes.

tastingProfile.style: The most powerful of Burgundy — iron fist in velvet glove

appellations.name: Vosne-Romanée
appellations.description: The most celebrated wine village on earth. The 1.8ha La Romanée-Conti Grand Cru produces perhaps the world's most expensive wine. La Tâche, Richebourg and Grands-Échézeaux are hardly less extraordinary.
appellations.climate: Continental, with Vougeot providing some wind protection.

tastingProfile.style: Transcendent — perfume, silk and eternal complexity

appellations.name: Meursault
appellations.description: The world's greatest village for white Burgundy. Incomparable buttery richness and hazelnut depth. No Grand Crus but 1er crus Perrières, Charmes and Genevrières are among Burgundy's most sought-after whites.
appellations.climate: Continental — south and east facing for perfect Chardonnay exposure.

tastingProfile.style: Buttery, nutty, richly textured white Burgundy

appellations.name: Chablis
appellations.description: The purest, most mineral Chardonnay. Kimmeridgian limestone — ancient seabed packed with oyster fossils — gives saline, oyster-shell character found nowhere else. No oak, no butter: pure terroir.
appellations.climate: Cold continental — the most northerly of Burgundy's sub-regions.

tastingProfile.style: The purest Chardonnay — steely, saline, no compromise

appellations.name: Puligny-Montrachet
appellations.description: The village that shares Le Montrachet — Alexandre Dumas said it should be drunk 'kneeling, with head bowed'. The four Grand Crus produce the world's greatest dry white wines of staggering richness, complexity and longevity.
appellations.climate: Continental — the best Montrachet plots face east-southeast at 250m altitude.

tastingProfile.style: The pinnacle of dry white wine — Montrachet Grand Cru

description: The world's most celebrated sparkling wine region. Chalk subsoils, cold northerly climate and the traditional method combine to create wines of unrivalled complexity and celebratory grandeur.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Dom Pérignon, the 17th-century Benedictine monk, did not invent champagne but pioneered blending and bottling techniques that defined it.

appellations.name: Montagne de Reims
appellations.description: The forested plateau south of Reims produces Pinot Noir of extraordinary backbone and red-fruit depth for champagne blending. Verzenay and Mailly are Grand Cru villages of legendary reputation.
appellations.climate: Cool continental — chalk subsoil drains well, retaining acidity.

tastingProfile.style: Pinot-driven, structured champagne — backbone and longevity

appellations.name: Côte des Blancs
appellations.description: The spiritual home of Blanc de Blancs champagne. Pure Chardonnay on chalk slopes south of Épernay produces wines of crystalline precision, delicate citrus and extraordinary ageing potential. Cramant, Avize and Le Mesnil-sur-Oger are the holy trinity.
appellations.climate: Cool continental — east-facing chalk slopes provide perfect Chardonnay conditions.

tastingProfile.style: Blanc de Blancs — crystalline purity, electric tension, infinite age

wineries.name: Salon
wineries.flagship: Salon Le Mesnil Blanc de Blancs

wineries.name: Pierre Péters
wineries.flagship: Cuvée de Réserve Blanc de Blancs

description: From the granitic terraces of the Northern Rhône to the garrigue-scented plains of the south. Syrah and Grenache reign supreme.

mythology.name: Hercules
mythology.role: Hero associated with the Rhône route
mythology.note: Greek traders from Massalia (Marseille) established viticulture along the Rhône in the 6th century BCE.

appellations.name: Hermitage
appellations.description: The great hill of Hermitage is a single granite massif producing Syrah of mythical stature. 19th-century négociants used 'Hermitaged' as a verb — meaning to blend in Hermitage to improve lesser wines. The whites (Marsanne/Roussanne) are among France's most long-lived.
appellations.climate: Continental — granite hill facing full south, Mistral wind desiccates grapes.

tastingProfile.style: Titanic Syrah — the greatest Northern Rhône expression

appellations.name: Côte-Rôtie
appellations.description: The 'Roasted Slope' — the most northerly and most elegant of the Northern Rhône. Up to 20% Viognier blended with Syrah adds extraordinary floral perfume. La Mouline, La Landonne and La Turque (Guigal's single vineyard wines) are modern legends.
appellations.climate: Continental — steep amphitheatre of schist and gneiss, Mistral protection.

tastingProfile.style: The most elegant Northern Rhône — Syrah perfumed with Viognier

wineries.name: E. Guigal
wineries.flagship: La Mouline / La Landonne / La Turque

appellations.name: Châteauneuf-du-Pape
appellations.description: Up to 18 grape varieties are permitted. The famous galets roulés (large smooth stones) radiate heat at night, producing wines of extraordinary richness and southern warmth. The first ever AOC wine law was written here in 1936.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — 3,000 hours of sunshine, strong Mistral wind.

tastingProfile.style: Southern warmth and garrigue — the most hedonistic of France

description: Spain's most famous wine region. Tempranillo aged in American and French oak produces wines of extraordinary complexity — from fresh 'Joven' to ancient 'Gran Reserva' aged a decade before release.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: Greek and Phoenician traders brought viticulture to Iberia from the 7th century BCE. The Romans later systematised production across Rioja.

appellations.name: Rioja Alta
appellations.description: The coolest and highest of Rioja's three sub-zones. Atlantic influence from the Cantabrian Mountains gives Rioja Alta its freshness and elegance. Home to the great historic bodegas — La Rioja Alta, Muga, Marqués de Murrieta.
appellations.climate: Continental-Atlantic — cool, wet influence from north; hot, dry influence from south.

tastingProfile.style: Rioja's most elegant sub-zone — freshness, precision, great age

appellations.name: Rioja Alavesa
appellations.description: Rioja Alavesa is the smallest sub-zone, with the highest altitude and most limestone-rich soils. Its wines are more structured and age-worthy than Rioja Baja, and its small-domaine culture has sparked a new-wave of artisanal producers.
appellations.climate: Continental with Atlantic influence — elevated, cooler than Baja.

tastingProfile.style: Structured, mineral Rioja — new-wave artisanal elegance

appellations.name: Priorat
appellations.description: Spain's most dramatic wine landscape — ancient Garnacha vines on vertical slate (llicorella) terraces. Two DOCas in Spain: Rioja and Priorat. The wines are intense, mineral, searingly concentrated with a slate-and-mineral backbone unique in the world.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean continental — extreme heat, no rainfall, poor slate soils.

tastingProfile.style: Spain's most intense — slate-mineral Garnacha of astonishing concentration

description: Spain's answer to Bordeaux. High-altitude plateau (800–1,000m) and cold nights produce Spain's most structured, age-worthy Tempranillo (Tinto Fino). Vega Sicilia is the country's most legendary estate.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Roman villas along the Duero produced wine for the legions. Evidence of 1st-century production found at Clunia.

appellations.name: Valbuena / Vega Sicilia
appellations.description: Spain's most legendary wine estate. Vega Sicilia Único — aged a minimum 10 years before release — is the country's most expensive and age-worthy wine. The Riberan landscape of limestone plateau and ancient vines is extraordinarily dramatic.
appellations.climate: Extreme continental — 450m altitude, -20°C winters, +40°C summers, dramatic diurnal range.

tastingProfile.style: Spain's most majestic — Tempranillo with Bordeaux soul, built for decades

appellations.name: Pesquera / Central Ribera
appellations.description: The heart of Ribera del Duero, centred on the village of Pesquera de Duero. Alejandro Fernández's Pesquera wines in the 1980s revealed the region's world-class potential to international audiences and triggered the modern Ribera boom.
appellations.climate: Extreme continental — high plateau, cold winters, very hot summers.

tastingProfile.style: Powerful, concentrated high-plateau Tempranillo

description: The heart of Italian fine wine. From Sangiovese's Renaissance heartland — Chianti Classico, Brunello di Montalcino, Vino Nobile — to the modern Supertuscans that shattered the old order.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: The Etruscans were making wine in Tuscany by the 8th century BCE and are credited with teaching the Romans viticulture.

appellations.name: Brunello di Montalcino
appellations.description: Italy's most celebrated red wine. 100% Sangiovese aged minimum 5 years (7 for Riserva) in oak. Montalcino's south-facing hills and dry, windy climate produce Sangiovese of extraordinary tannic grip and longevity — the 'Barolo of Tuscany'.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean continental — hot, dry summers. 564m altitude on Monte Amiata's slopes.

tastingProfile.style: Italy's most age-worthy — iron tannins, eternal rose petal, majestic

appellations.name: Chianti Classico
appellations.description: Between Florence and Siena lies the historic core of Chianti. The 'Gallo Nero' (Black Rooster) Gran Selezione wines are now among Italy's most compelling. Sangiovese on galestro schist produces wines of cherry, dried herbs and vivid acidity.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — hot dry summers, cold winters, 300–600m altitude.

tastingProfile.style: The heartbeat of Tuscany — vivid, food-loving Sangiovese

appellations.name: Bolgheri (Sassicaia)
appellations.description: The birthplace of the Supertuscan revolution. Sassicaia — dismissed by Italian law as humble table wine when first released in 1968 — changed everything. Today Bolgheri produces Italy's most internationally celebrated Bordeaux-style blends from coastal hillside terroir.
appellations.climate: Maritime Mediterranean — sea breezes from the Tyrrhenian temper the heat.

tastingProfile.style: Italy's Bordeaux — power, elegance, the Supertuscan benchmark

description: The 'Wine Cellar of Italy' at the foot of the Alps. Barolo and Barbaresco — made from Nebbiolo — are Italy's most profound reds. But Barbera, Dolcetto, Moscato and the unique Arneis offer extraordinary breadth.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: The Ligurian peoples of ancient Piedmont were documented as wine drinkers by Greek historian Thucydides in the 5th century BCE.

appellations.name: Barolo
appellations.description: 'The King of Italian Wines'. 100% Nebbiolo from the Langhe hills. Two styles war here: the traditionalist (long maceration, large Slavonian oak, 20-year minimum before peak) versus the modernist (shorter maceration, French barriques, earlier accessibility). Both produce wines of towering complexity.
appellations.climate: Continental with Alpine influence — cold winters, hot summers, frequent autumn fog (nebbia = fog).

tastingProfile.style: King of Italian wines — Rose, tar, iron; built for decades

appellations.name: Barbaresco
appellations.description: 'The Queen of Italian Wines'. Nebbiolo on Tortonian marl soils produces wines slightly lighter and more fragrant than Barolo — aged only 2 years (4 for Riserva). Gaja single-vineyard wines — Sorì Tildin, Costa Russi, Sorì San Lorenzo — are among Italy's most expensive.
appellations.climate: Continental — Tanaro river moderation, slightly warmer than Barolo.

tastingProfile.style: Queen to Barolo's King — more fragrant, more feminine, earlier ready

appellations.name: Moscato d'Asti
appellations.description: Italy's most delightful light sweet wine — low alcohol (5–6.5%), gently sparkling (frizzante), intensely perfumed. Produced only from Moscato Bianco (White Muscat) in the hills around Canelli. An afternoon of apricot, orange blossom and pure joy.
appellations.climate: Continental — Canelli hills, morning fog, perfect Muscat conditions.

tastingProfile.style: Italy's most joyful wine — perfumed, sweet, feather-light

description: The world's most dramatic Riesling vineyards — slate slopes at 60–70° gradient above the meandering river. Mosel Riesling is the world's lightest, most ethereal wine: 7% alcohol, electric acidity and 50-year ageing potential.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: The Romans planted Mosel's first vineyards c.300 CE. Ausonius praised 'the vines of the Mosella' in his famous poem.

appellations.name: Bernkastel / Mittelmosel
appellations.description: The heart of the Mosel. The Doctor vineyard at Bernkastel is Germany's most famous individual site. Blue-grey Devonian slate retains solar heat on near-vertical slopes, producing Riesling of otherworldly mineral precision — peach, slate and petrol intertwined.
appellations.climate: Cold continental — extreme vineyard gradient maximises solar gain. River loop creates warmth.

tastingProfile.style: The most ethereal wine — featherweight, electric, eternal Riesling

appellations.name: Saar
appellations.description: The Saar tributary produces Mosel's most austere and steely Rieslings. In great vintages the wines transcend — gossamer delicacy of almost unbearable beauty. The Scharzhofberg is the Riesling world's Romanée-Conti.
appellations.climate: Coldest of the Mosel tributaries — extreme Continental, late harvests essential.

tastingProfile.style: Mosel's most austere — steely, precise, coldly beautiful

description: One of the world's oldest demarcated wine regions (1756). The Douro produces both the magnificent Port wine and, increasingly, dry table wines of extraordinary character from ancient indigenous varieties.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: The Lusitanians were making wine in the Douro before Roman arrival. The Portuguese word 'vinho' derives directly from the Latin 'vinum'.

appellations.name: Vintage Port
appellations.description: The aristocrat of fortified wines. Declared only in the finest years, vintage Port is bottled after 2 years and aged for 20–50+ years in bottle. The Symington family (Graham's, Dow's, Warre's) and Fladgate Partnership (Taylor's, Fonseca) have defined the style for generations.
appellations.climate: Extreme continental — Atlantic blocked by mountains. +40°C summer, -5°C winter.

tastingProfile.style: The greatest fortified wine — magnificent structure, eternal ageing

appellations.name: Douro Superior (Table Wine)
appellations.description: The revolution in Douro table wines began with Barca Velha (first released 1952) and accelerated with Niepoort's Redoma and Charme. Ancient indigenous varieties on schist terraces produce dry reds of startling complexity — Portugal's answer to great Burgundy.
appellations.climate: Extreme continental — upper Douro, hottest and most arid. Ancient ungrafted vines survive.

tastingProfile.style: Portugal's greatest dry red — schist minerality, ancient vine intensity

description: Since the 1976 Judgement of Paris, Napa has been the New World's benchmark for Cabernet Sauvignon — opulent, powerful, built to age.

mythology.name: Coyote Spirit
mythology.note: The Wappo people inhabited Napa Valley for thousands of years before European settlement.

appellations.name: Rutherford
appellations.description: 'Rutherford dust' — a silky, earthy mid-palate texture unique to its benchland gravel soils. Some of Napa's oldest estates sit here, producing Cabernet of extraordinary finesse.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — warm days, cold nights, fog from San Pablo Bay.

tastingProfile.style: The epitome of Napa — dusty, plush, eternally complex

appellations.name: Oakville
appellations.description: Arguably Napa's finest AVA — home to Opus One and Screaming Eagle. Well-drained benchland soils and optimal sun exposure produce Cabernet of extraordinary balance between power and finesse.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — warm, protected from coast. Classic Napa fog-free ripening.

tastingProfile.style: Napa's summit — power and finesse in perfect balance

appellations.name: Stags Leap District
appellations.description: The AVA that put California on the world map at the 1976 Paris Tasting. Volcanic palisades retain heat and protect from wind, yielding silky, plush Cabernet of remarkable elegance.
appellations.climate: Slightly cooler south Napa — volcanic palisades create a heat trap.

tastingProfile.style: Silky, approachable — Napa's most elegant Cabernet

appellations.name: Santa Barbara County
appellations.description: California's most exciting cool-climate region. Transverse mountain ranges channel cold Pacific air inland. Made famous by the film Sideways, Sta. Rita Hills produces Pinot Noir that rivals Burgundy in elegance.
appellations.climate: Cool maritime — transverse valleys funnel Pacific fog and wind. Extreme diurnal variation.

tastingProfile.style: Cool-climate California — Burgundian elegance meets Pacific freshness

description: At Andean altitude, Malbec found its spiritual home. Mendoza's dry desert air, intense UV and cold nights produce wines of extraordinary depth and concentration.

mythology.name: Pachamama
mythology.note: Still honoured at harvest with offerings of food, coca leaves and chicha.

appellations.name: Luján de Cuyo
appellations.description: Argentina's first and only DOC. Alluvial soils at 900–1,100m produce Malbec of extraordinary structure. The DOC guarantees organic practices and maximum yields. Home to Argentina's most prestigious producers.
appellations.climate: High desert — arid, intense UV, extreme diurnal variation (18–22°C daily swing).

tastingProfile.style: Argentina's finest Malbec — structured, complex, built to age

appellations.name: Valle de Uco
appellations.description: Argentina's most exciting wine frontier. At 1,000–1,500m in the Andean foothills, rocky soils and extreme cold nights produce wines of mineral precision and freshness unmatched at lower elevations.
appellations.climate: Extreme high desert — highest UV, coldest nights, dramatic diurnal swings.

tastingProfile.style: High-altitude precision — mineral, fresh, Argentina's emerging finest

description: The Cape's fine wine heartland — sun-drenched mountain flanks producing richly textured reds, world-class Chenin Blanc and home to Pinotage, South Africa's unique grape.

mythology.name: Qamata
mythology.note: The Xhosa are the indigenous custodians of the Western Cape. Qamata governs life's cycles and harvests.

appellations.name: Helderberg
appellations.description: Stellenbosch's finest terroir for red wines. Decomposed granite, cooling ocean breeze from False Bay, and altitude produce Cabernet and Shiraz of extraordinary elegance and structure.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — hot dry summers, cool mountain breeze from False Bay.

tastingProfile.style: Cape's finest — fynbos-scented, structured mountain reds

appellations.name: Simonsberg-Stellenbosch
appellations.description: Kanonkop estate sits here — producing Pinotage and Paul Sauer (Bordeaux blend) of world-class pedigree. South Africa's most powerful and concentrated reds come from these north-facing Simonsberg slopes.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — sheltered north-facing slopes with longer ripening season.

tastingProfile.style: South Africa's most powerful — Pinotage and Bordeaux blends

description: One of the world's oldest wine regions. The Phoenicians invented wine commerce and the Bekaa's soils have yielded wine for 5,000 years. Home to the greatest Roman temple of Bacchus.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: The Temple of Bacchus at Baalbek, c.150 CE, is the world's best-preserved Roman temple — a monument to wine in Lebanon's heartland.

mythology.name: El
mythology.note: The Phoenicians were the ancient world's greatest wine traders, spreading viticulture from Canaan across the Mediterranean from 3000 BCE.

appellations.name: Château Musar (Ghazir/Bekaa)
appellations.description: Château Musar continued production through Lebanon's civil war, bottling the 1984 in a besieged country. Using 19th-century methods and aged years before release, Musar Rouge is one of the world's most individual and age-worthy reds.
appellations.climate: High-altitude Mediterranean — 1,000m elevation, no irrigation needed.

tastingProfile.style: Ancient, exotic — the Phoenician inheritance, unchanged

description: Home to the world's oldest Shiraz vines — some ungrafted, 150+ years old. The Barossa produces rich, hedonistic Shiraz unlike anywhere else on earth.

mythology.name: Parra-Wirra spirit
mythology.note: The Kaurna people inhabited the Barossa Valley for thousands of years. 'Barossa' may derive from Borossa, a Silesian place name brought by German settlers in 1842.

appellations.name: Barossa Valley Shiraz
appellations.description: Old-vine Barossa Shiraz — from vines 80–160 years old — produces wines of extraordinary concentration, power and texture. Penfolds Grange has elevated the Barossa to global reverence. The 'GSM' blend (Grenache-Shiraz-Mourvèdre) is an Australian original.
appellations.climate: Hot Mediterranean — warm, dry growing season; cold winters provide vine rest.

tastingProfile.style: Hedonistic, concentrated old-vine Shiraz — the New World's answer to Hermitage

appellations.name: Eden Valley Riesling
appellations.description: High on the Barossa Ranges, Eden Valley's cool climate and quartz-rich soils produce Australia's most revered Riesling — Henschke's Julius is the benchmark. Intensely limey and mineral in youth, developing glorious petrol complexity with age.
appellations.climate: Cool — altitude 380–550m moderates the Barossa heat dramatically.

tastingProfile.style: Australia's great Riesling — steely lime, austere, magnificent with age

description: New Zealand's wine heartland. Marlborough reinvented Sauvignon Blanc — the pungent, tropical, electric style that took the world by storm. Cloudy Bay's 1985 launch changed international wine forever.

mythology.name: Maui
mythology.role: Demigod who fished up the North Island
mythology.note: Marlborough was part of the South Island — Te Waka o Maui — in Māori tradition, the canoe from which Maui fished.

appellations.name: Wairau Valley Sauvignon Blanc
appellations.description: The valley floor where Marlborough's Sauvignon Blanc story began. Stony, free-draining soils and long sunshine hours produce the world's most recognisable Sauvignon Blanc: passionfruit, capsicum and fresh-cut grass in irresistible abundance.
appellations.climate: Cool maritime — long dry summers, cool nights preserving acidity. Strong UV intensity.

tastingProfile.style: The world's most recognisable Sauvignon Blanc — explosive, tropical, vivid

appellations.name: Awatere Valley Pinot Noir
appellations.description: South of the Wairau, the Awatere Valley is cooler and more austere — its Pinot Noir has red-cherry delicacy closer to Burgundy than the riper Wairau style. Increasingly recognised as Marlborough's most refined sub-region.
appellations.climate: Coolest of Marlborough's sub-regions — strong Northerly winds create stress and concentration.

tastingProfile.style: Refined, Burgundian-tinged Pinot Noir — Marlborough's cool secret

description: The most dramatic and revered of Austria's wine regions. Steep terraced vineyards above the Danube gorge produce Grüner Veltliner and Riesling of world-class mineral intensity.

mythology.name: Hermes
mythology.note: Roman merchants traded Wachau wines along the Danube trade routes from the 1st century CE. The Babenberg monastery at Melk bottled wine continuously from the 11th century.

appellations.name: Spitz / Wachau Grüner Veltliner
appellations.description: Wachau's signature grape, Grüner Veltliner — white pepper, citrus, minerals — reaches its apotheosis here on steep primary rock terraces. The Wachau's three quality tiers — Steinfeder, Federspiel, Smaragd — define Austrian wine culture.
appellations.climate: Continental — warm Pannonian influence from east meets cool Alpine air from west.

tastingProfile.style: Wachau's heartbeat — white pepper, minerals, Danube-pure Grüner

description: The Wine of Kings and King of Wines. Tokaji Aszú — made from noble-rotted Furmint berries — was the world's first classified wine (1730) and inspired Sauternes.

mythology.name: Zalmoxis
mythology.role: God of Immortality and the Vine
mythology.note: Dacian tribes in the Tokaj region venerated wine as a sacred gift. Louis XIV of France called Tokaji 'the wine of kings, the king of wines'.

appellations.name: Tokaji Aszú
appellations.description: The world's first officially classified wine region (1730 — decades before Bordeaux). Aszú berries shrivelled by noble rot are added by 'puttony' measure to dry base wine, producing increasingly rich levels: 3-puttonyos to the legendary Eszencia (barely fermented, 250g/l residual sugar).
appellations.climate: Continental — autumn fogs from the Tisza and Bodrog rivers create perfect botrytis conditions.

tastingProfile.style: Liquid amber — sweet, electric acidity, ancient and complex

description: Volcanic island with ancient Assyrtiko vines trained in kouloura baskets to protect from the Aegean wind. Possibly the world's oldest continuously cultivated wine region.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: Santorini's Bronze Age Akrotiri settlement (destroyed c.1627 BCE) contains the world's oldest wine press, dated to 1700–1600 BCE.

appellations.name: Santorini Assyrtiko
appellations.description: Ancient Assyrtiko vines — some 400 years old, ungrafted (phylloxera cannot survive in volcanic ash soil) — produce one of the world's most electric dry whites. Volcanic pumice and basalt, constant Aegean winds, and near-desert conditions concentrate the grapes intensely.
appellations.climate: Semi-arid Mediterranean — extreme sun, constant northerly Meltemi wind, volcanic pumice soil.

tastingProfile.style: Electric, saline, volcanic — Greece's most thrilling white

description: The cradle of wine. Archaeological evidence confirms wine-making in Kakheti as early as 6000 BCE — making Georgia the oldest known wine-producing region on earth. The qvevri (clay amphora) is UNESCO-listed.

mythology.name: Amirani
mythology.note: Georgian mythology intertwines wine with civilization itself. The qvevri burial of wine was a sacred act symbolising death and resurrection.

appellations.name: Kakheti Rkatsiteli (Amber Wine)
appellations.description: Georgia invented orange/amber wine 8,000 years ago. Rkatsiteli fermented and macerated for 6 months on skins in buried qvevri produces amber wines of extraordinary tannin structure, dried fruit and spice complexity — unlike anything in Western winemaking. Saperavi, Georgia's great red, produces intensely coloured, age-worthy reds.
appellations.climate: Continental — Alazani valley, Caucasus mountain protection, hot summers, cold winters.

tastingProfile.style: 8,000 years of history in a glass — amber, tannic, wholly unique

description: Chile's most prestigious red wine valley. Carmenère — lost from Bordeaux in 1867, rediscovered in Chile in 1994 — found its spiritual home here. Lapostolle and Casa Lapostolle produce internationally acclaimed reds.

mythology.name: Ngenechen
mythology.role: Supreme Being, Guardian of People and Harvest
mythology.note: The Mapuche people cultivated the Colchagua Valley long before Spanish colonisation. Wine arrived with the Jesuits in the 17th century.

appellations.name: Colchagua Carmenère
appellations.description: Carmenère was mistaken for Merlot for over a century in Chile. Only in 1994 was it identified as the 'lost' Bordeaux variety. On clay soils with abundant sunshine, Colchagua Carmenère produces wines of velvety, dark-fruited richness with a distinctive herbaceous, chocolate note.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — hot, dry summers; Pacific influence moderates; Andean diurnal range.

tastingProfile.style: Chile's signature — velvety Carmenère, uniquely South American

description: France's most Germanic wine region — Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris and Muscat reach unique ripeness under the Vosges rain shadow. Alsace is the only French region that labels by grape variety, not appellation.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: The Via Alsatia Roman road linked Alsace's vineyards to the Rhine trade routes from the 1st century CE.

appellations.name: Alsace Grand Cru Riesling
appellations.description: Alsace Riesling is the region's intellectual backbone — dry, powerful, built to age. The 51 Grand Cru sites, each with defined geology, produce Riesling of extraordinary site-specific character. Rangen de Thann (volcanic), Schlossberg (granite) and Rosacker (Kimmeridgian) are the most celebrated.
appellations.climate: Continental, protected from rain by the Vosges mountains — France's driest wine region.

tastingProfile.style: Alsace's king — dry, powerful, petrol-kissed with infinite age

wineries.name: Domaine Zind-Humbrecht
wineries.flagship: Riesling Rangen de Thann Clos Saint-Urbain

appellations.name: Alsace Gewurztraminer
appellations.description: The world's most flamboyant white wine. Gewurztraminer from Alsace — rose petals, lychee, ginger, cinnamon — is instantly identifiable. The grape's low acidity and high alcohol make it uniquely heady. Vendange Tardive versions are among the world's great sweet wines.
appellations.climate: Continental, very warm — Gewurztraminer needs heat for phenolic ripeness.

tastingProfile.style: The most flamboyant white — exotic, heady, unmistakable

description: France's largest wine region and the world's most ancient continuously cultivated vineyards. Greek colonists planted Massalia (Marseille) and the Hérault Valley from 600 BCE. Today it produces everything from hedonistic Grenache blends to France's finest sparkling Crémant.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: Greek colonists from Phocaea planted Languedoc's first vines c.600 BCE, establishing Massalia (Marseille) as the Mediterranean's greatest wine port.

appellations.name: Pic Saint-Loup
appellations.description: The Languedoc's most prestigious appellation — a dramatic limestone peak north of Montpellier sheltering vines from the Mediterranean heat. Syrah and Grenache produce wines of extraordinary freshness and garrigue complexity, far more elegant than typical southern France.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean with mountain freshness — altitude moderates heat significantly.

tastingProfile.style: Languedoc's finest — garrigue-scented elegance from a limestone peak

appellations.name: Banyuls
appellations.description: France's greatest fortified wine — and the only one that pairs magnificently with chocolate. Ancient Grenache vines on near-vertical black schist terraces above the Mediterranean at the Spanish border. The Rancio style (oxidative ageing) produces wines of extraordinary walnut and coffee complexity.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean extreme — dry, hot, Tramontane wind. No irrigation ever.

tastingProfile.style: France's Vino de Jerez — aged Grenache, chocolate-perfect

description: Sherry is arguably the world's most underrated and misunderstood wine. From bone-dry Fino and electric Manzanilla to the rich, caramelised Pedro Ximénez, the solera system produces wines of incomparable complexity. Phoenicians planted Jerez's first vines around 1100 BCE.

mythology.name: Melqart
mythology.role: God of the sea and commerce
mythology.note: Phoenicians from Tyre founded Gadir (Cádiz) c.1100 BCE and planted the albariza chalk soils of Jerez — making this one of the world's oldest continuously cultivated wine regions.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Roman Gades (Cádiz) was the westernmost major city of the Empire. Julius Caesar was said to have wept at Gadir before Hercules' statue — next to vineyards.

appellations.name: Fino / Manzanilla
appellations.description: The world's most food-friendly wine. Fino Sherry and its coastal cousin Manzanilla (from Sanlúcar de Barrameda, where sea air creates extra salinity) are aged biologically under a layer of flor yeast, producing astonishingly dry, saline, yeasty wines of extraordinary complexity. Drunk cold as an aperitif with jamón ibérico they are transformative.
appellations.climate: Hot Mediterranean — unique albariza chalk retains winter rain for summer. Atlantic influence from Sanlúcar.

tastingProfile.style: Bone-dry, saline, biological ageing — the most unique white wine

appellations.name: Pedro Ximénez (PX)
appellations.description: The world's richest wine. PX grapes are sun-dried on esparto mats until they become raisins, then pressed to produce liquid so sweet and concentrated it pours like molasses — 400–500g/l residual sugar. Poured over vanilla ice cream it is transcendent. Very old PX (50+ years, from Montilla-Moriles) reaches almost mythical complexity.
appellations.climate: Extreme heat — Montilla-Moriles is hotter and drier than Jerez.

tastingProfile.style: The world's sweetest wine — molasses, fig and infinite complexity

description: Spain's Atlantic northwest — Celtic, rainy, granite. Albariño from the Rías Baixas sub-region has become Spain's most celebrated white wine: peachy, saline, electric with Atlantic minerals.

mythology.name: Santiago (Saint James)
mythology.note: The Camino de Santiago pilgrimage route passes through Galicia. Medieval pilgrims brought winemaking knowledge that transformed the region's viticulture from the 9th century.

appellations.name: Rías Baixas Albariño
appellations.description: Spain's finest white wine. Albariño on granite soils above the Atlantic rías (drowned river valleys) produces wines of extraordinary peach-and-citrus fragrance, saline minerality and refreshing acidity. The Val do Salnés sub-zone is the finest — granitic, windswept, oceanic.
appellations.climate: Atlantic oceanic — cool, wet, high humidity. Pergola-trained vines lifted off damp granite soils.

tastingProfile.style: Spain's most refreshing white — peachy, saline, Atlantic-pure

description: Italy's most productive wine region — home to Amarone, one of the world's most powerful dry reds, the delicate Valpolicella, crisp Soave, and the celebratory Prosecco. The Veneto's diversity is unmatched in the Italian peninsula.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: Euganei and Veneti tribes were documented as skilled winemakers by Greek historians from the 5th century BCE. The Roman city of Verona became the Empire's great wine trading centre.

appellations.name: Amarone della Valpolicella
appellations.description: One of the world's most unique wines. Grapes are laid on bamboo racks (arele) for 3–4 months to dry (appassimento), concentrating sugars and flavours. The resulting wine is fermented to dryness — 16–17% alcohol — producing a wine of staggering richness, dark chocolate, cherry and bitter almond complexity. Amarone was 'invented' when a Recioto (sweet version) was accidentally fermented to dryness in the 1930s.
appellations.climate: Continental Veronese hills — Lake Garda moderates temperature. Cold winters essential for vine rest.

tastingProfile.style: The most powerful dry red — dried grapes, 17% alcohol, iron will

appellations.name: Soave Classico
appellations.description: At its best — from the ancient volcanic Classico zone around the medieval castle — Soave is one of Italy's most elegant whites: almond, white peach, mineral, with a distinctive bitter-almond finish. Unfortunately Soave's reputation was damaged by industrial production; the Classico DOC is now reclaiming its heritage.
appellations.climate: Continental — volcanic basalt hillsides around Soave castle. Lake Garda influence.

tastingProfile.style: Italy's underrated white — almond, mineral, castle-hill elegance

appellations.name: Prosecco di Conegliano Valdobbiadene
appellations.description: The world's most consumed sparkling wine — and at its finest (Cartizze, single-vineyard Rive, Extra Brut) one of the most charming. The steep UNESCO-listed hillside vineyards of Valdobbiadene produce Glera grapes of real character. Prosecco Superiore DOCG is fundamentally different from the mass-market DOC.
appellations.climate: Humid continental — Dolomite foothills, morning fog, afternoon sun. Perfect for Glera's fresh acidity.

tastingProfile.style: Celebratory freshness — pear, flowers, the world's aperitivo

description: The Mediterranean's ancient wine island — Greeks called Sicily Oenotria (Land of Wine). Etna's volcanic slopes produce some of Italy's most thrilling new-wave wines, while Marsala remains one of history's great fortified wines.

mythology.name: Dionysus
mythology.note: Greek colonists from Corinth founded Syracuse in 734 BCE and systematically planted vines across Sicily. The island became known throughout the ancient world as a land of extraordinary wine.

mythology.name: Demeter
mythology.note: Sicily was sacred to Demeter — her daughter Persephone was abducted from Sicily's plains. The island was the breadbasket (and wine cellar) of ancient Greece.

appellations.name: Etna Rosso / Bianco
appellations.description: Italy's most exciting wine discovery of the 21st century. Ancient Nerello Mascalese vines on black volcanic ash at 400–1,000m altitude produce wines of Burgundian delicacy and site-specific mineral complexity. Each contrada (village) on Etna's slopes expresses volcanic terroir differently. The whites (Carricante) are saline, mineral and age magnificently.
appellations.climate: Volcanic Mediterranean — altitude creates dramatic diurnal range. Vines grow in ancient black lava flows.

tastingProfile.style: Italy's Burgundy — volcanic minerality, ancient Nerello, electric acidity

appellations.name: Marsala
appellations.description: Created by English merchant John Woodhouse in 1796 when his ship was forced into Marsala harbour by a storm. Fortified with grape spirit for the voyage to England, the wine became a sensation. Vergine/Soleras Marsala — bone-dry, aged 10+ years — is one of Italy's most complex wines. The sweet 'Fine' style is cooking wine; the aged dry style is transcendent.
appellations.climate: Hot Mediterranean — intense sun on Sicily's western tip, sea winds from Africa.

tastingProfile.style: Sicily's great fortified — dry Vergine, sherry-like complexity

description: Germany's warmest and most diverse wine region — the Pfalz produces everything from powerful dry Riesling GGs to opulent Spätburgunder (Pinot Noir). The Mittelhaardt is Germany's 'Côte d'Or'.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: The Via Agrippa Roman road ran through the Pfalz, and Roman villae vineariae (wine estates) dotted the Haardt mountains from the 1st century CE. The remains of the Roman wine press at Bad Dürkheim are among Germany's most important viticultural archaeological sites.

appellations.name: Forst / Deidesheim Riesling GG
appellations.description: The Mittelhaardt villages of Forst and Deidesheim produce Germany's most powerful dry Rieslings outside the Mosel. Basalt outcrops in the Forster Kirchenstück and Jesuitengarten retain heat to produce Riesling of tropical richness combined with Rhenish mineral precision. Dry Pfalz Riesling GGs are Germany's most food-versatile wines.
appellations.climate: Continental — warmest of Germany's regions, protected by the Haardt mountains.

tastingProfile.style: Germany's most powerful dry Riesling — tropical, mineral, compelling

description: Where Riesling was born. The south-facing Rhine bend at Rüdesheim creates unique conditions for Riesling of unrivalled complexity. Johannisberg — where Spätlese was 'invented' in 1775 — is Germany's most historic wine estate.

mythology.name: Hildegard von Bingen
mythology.note: The great medieval mystic Hildegard von Bingen (1098–1179) managed vineyards on the Rhine and wrote extensively about wine's medicinal properties in her Physica.

appellations.name: Schloss Johannisberg
appellations.description: Germany's most historically significant wine estate — continuously producing since the Benedictine monks planted Riesling here in 1100 CE. The 1775 late harvest 'accident' that created Spätlese (the messenger carrying permission to harvest was delayed, grapes rotted nobly) transformed German winemaking. Schloss Johannisberg Rieslings are benchmarks for Rheingau elegance.
appellations.climate: South-facing Rhine loop — maximum solar exposure, Rhine moderates temperature extremes.

tastingProfile.style: Rheingau's crown jewel — historic, elegant, deceptively ageable

description: Portugal's largest and greenest wine region. Vinho Verde ('green wine') refers not to colour but youth — the wines are bottled young, with natural spritz and low alcohol. Alvarinho (Albariño) from Monção e Melgaço is Portugal's finest white.

mythology.name: São Martinho
mythology.role: Patron Saint of Harvest and Wine
mythology.note: St Martin's Day (November 11) marks the traditional tasting of the year's new wine in northern Portugal, a celebration dating to medieval Benedictine viticulture.

appellations.name: Alvarinho de Monção e Melgaço
appellations.description: Portugal's finest sub-region for white wine. Alvarinho (the same grape as Spanish Albariño) achieves greater ripeness and body in Monção's continental climate than in Galicia. The wines have more texture and age better — Portugal's answer to white Burgundy's richness combined with Atlantic freshness.
appellations.climate: Continental Atlantic — warmer and drier than the rest of Vinho Verde. Granite-rich soils.

tastingProfile.style: Portugal's finest white — richer than Galician Albariño, minerally complex

description: Portugal's vast, sunbaked interior — cork forests, wheat fields and ancient Alentejo wines. Esporão and Herdade do Esporão have led a modern wine revolution in one of Europe's last great wine frontiers.

mythology.name: Bacchus
mythology.note: Évora, the Roman city of Liberalitas Julia, was a major wine production centre. Enormous Roman wine-pressing facilities have been excavated throughout the Alentejo.

appellations.name: Alentejo Tinto
appellations.description: Alentejo reds — deeply coloured, ripe, fruit-forward — have become Portugal's most internationally successful wines after Port. The extreme heat produces wines of concentrated berry fruit, tempered by modern temperature-controlled winemaking. The Herdade do Esporão estate covers 1,800 hectares of cork-strewn plains.
appellations.climate: Extreme continental Mediterranean — +40°C summers, almost no rain. Portugal's hottest wine region.

tastingProfile.style: Portugal's sun-baked interior — rich, plummy, modern and approachable

description: South Africa's most exciting wine revolution. Eben Sadie and the Swartland Independent Producers transformed this 'wheat country' into the Cape's most dynamic wine region — old-vine Chenin Blanc, Syrah and Grenache blends of world-class pedigree.

mythology.name: Qamata
mythology.note: The Khoikhoi 'Goringhaicona' people inhabited the Swartland long before Dutch colonisation in 1652. Their cattle herding paths became the wine roads of the Western Cape.

appellations.name: Swartland Old-Vine Chenin Blanc
appellations.description: The Swartland revolution was built on ancient Chenin Blanc bush vines — some 40–60 years old, dry-farmed on schist and granite. Sadie Family's Palladius and Eben Sadie's blends inspired a generation of winemakers to explore the Cape's forgotten old-vine treasures. These Chenins rival the Loire Valley's finest.
appellations.climate: Hot, dry Mediterranean — Cape Doctor sea breeze crucial. No irrigation.

tastingProfile.style: Africa's greatest Chenin — old vines, schist minerals, profound and complex

description: America's Burgundy. David Lett ('Papa Pinot') planted Oregon's first Pinot Noir in 1965; in 1979 his Eyrie Vineyards Pinot beat Burgundy's Drouhin in a Paris tasting. The Dundee Hills' red volcanic Jory soil is uniquely suited to the world's most temperamental grape.

mythology.name: Coyote
mythology.note: The Kalapuya people managed the Willamette Valley with controlled burns for thousands of years, creating the open prairies that European settlers converted to vineyards.

appellations.name: Dundee Hills Pinot Noir
appellations.description: Oregon's most celebrated AVA. The distinctive red Jory volcanic soil — iron-rich, perfectly drained — produces Pinot Noir of extraordinary raspberry and earthy complexity. Domaine Drouhin Oregon (established 1987 by Robert Drouhin's daughter Véronique) confirmed Oregon's Burgundian calling.
appellations.climate: Cool maritime — rainy winters, warm dry summers. Maritime air from the Coast Range. Long cool growing season.

tastingProfile.style: America's Burgundy — earthy, red-fruited, genuine Pinot soul

description: Washington State's Cabernet-and-Syrah heartland. The desert climate east of the Cascades produces concentrated, structured reds utterly different from California — leaner, more European in style, with an electric acidity that gives exceptional longevity.

mythology.name: Coyote
mythology.note: The Cayuse people inhabited the Walla Walla valley for thousands of years. Their name for the area — 'Walla Walla' means 'many waters' — refers to the rivers that support viticulture.

appellations.name: Walla Walla Cabernet Sauvignon
appellations.description: Washington's finest AVA. High desert plateau at 300–450m with sandy loam soils over caliche rock. Long summer days (17 hours of sunlight at midsummer), cold nights, and near-zero disease pressure produce Cabernet of extraordinary concentration and longevity — darker and more structured than Napa, with a signature minty-herb note.
appellations.climate: High desert continental — intense sun, cold nights, almost no disease pressure. Irrigation essential.

tastingProfile.style: Washington's powerhouse — dark, structured, mint-cedar Cabernet

description: Chile's cool-climate revolution. Pablo Morandé planted Casablanca's first vines in 1982 despite conventional wisdom that the coastal fog was too cold. Today Casablanca produces Chile's finest Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc — and increasingly, world-class Pinot Noir.

mythology.name: Machi
mythology.role: Spiritual healer and mediator with nature spirits
mythology.note: The Mapuche people's deep connection to the coastal ranges of central Chile is reflected in Casablanca's wine culture — many producers now honour indigenous land relationships in their farming practices.

appellations.name: Casablanca Chardonnay / Sauvignon Blanc
appellations.description: Chile's most prestigious cool-climate appellation. Pacific fog rolls through gaps in the coastal range every morning, cooling the valley dramatically. Chardonnay achieves Burgundian elegance and Sauvignon Blanc rivals Marlborough for freshness and precision. Concha y Toro's Amelia and Viña Mar are benchmarks.
appellations.climate: Cool maritime — Pacific fog and cold Humboldt Current create Burgundy-like conditions at 33°S.

tastingProfile.style: Chile's cool-climate answer to Chablis and Marlborough

description: The original home of Malbec — known here as Côt or Auxerrois. Cahors 'Black Wine' was shipped to England and Bordeaux for centuries as a blending grape. Today it produces France's deepest, most tannic red wine — a world away from Argentine Malbec.

mythology.name: Divona
mythology.note: The city of Cahors was named from the sacred spring Divona Cadurcorum, worshipped by the Cadurci Gauls. Roman viticulture spread along the Lot river valley from the 1st century BCE.

appellations.name: Cahors Malbec (Côt)
appellations.description: French Malbec is a completely different wine from Argentine — harder, more austere, more tannic. The limestone causse plateau and river terraces of the Lot Valley produce Malbec of almost impenetrable colour and formidable structure. Great Cahors (Château du Cèdre, Clos Triguedina) can age 20–30 years to extraordinary complexity.
appellations.climate: Continental with Atlantic and Mediterranean influences — significant vintage variation.

tastingProfile.style: Malbec's Old World home — inky, austere, built for decades

description: Germany's most geologically diverse wine region — 26 different soil types in a small river valley. Emrich-Schönleber and Schäfer-Fröhlich have elevated the Nahe to world-class status with Rieslings that combine Mosel delicacy with Rheingau power.

mythology.name: Nahe river spirit
mythology.note: The Nahe (from Celtic 'Nava' meaning 'river') was a sacred waterway in pre-Roman Celtic and Germanic religion.

appellations.name: Monzingen / Niederhausen Riesling
appellations.description: The upper Nahe villages of Monzingen and Niederhausen produce Rieslings on volcanic porphyry (Hermannshöhle) and blue slate (Halenberg) soils of astonishing mineral complexity. Emrich-Schönleber's Halenberg GG and Schäfer-Fröhlich's Felseneck are among Germany's most sought-after wines.
appellations.climate: Continental with some Atlantic influence — more rainfall than Mosel, diverse soils create complexity.

tastingProfile.style: Germany's most complex geology in a glass — volcanic, electric Riesling

description: Just 40km south of Adelaide, McLaren Vale's Mediterranean climate and ancient soils produce South Australia's most hedonistic Shiraz and GSM blends. D'Arenberg and Clarendon Hills have given the region international status.

mythology.name: Tjilbruke
mythology.role: Ancestor spirit of the healing springs
mythology.note: The Kaurna people's Tjilbruke Dreaming Track runs through McLaren Vale to the coast. Tjilbruke, a great ancestor, created the freshwater springs of the Fleurieu Peninsula with his tears.

appellations.name: McLaren Vale Shiraz
appellations.description: McLaren Vale's ancient ironstone and sandy soils, combined with sea breezes from Gulf St Vincent, produce Shiraz of extraordinary complexity — more structured and savoury than Barossa, with a characteristic chocolate-and-licorice depth. Old vine Grenache from 80-year-old bush vines rivals the finest Châteauneuf.
appellations.climate: Mediterranean — warm, dry. Gulf St Vincent sea breeze critical for freshness.

tastingProfile.style: Seductive, chocolate-rich Shiraz — savoury and hedonistic

description: The world's southernmost wine region — and its most dramatic. Surrounded by mountains, Central Otago's continental climate (rare in New Zealand) produces Pinot Noir of extraordinary power and colour intensity. Its gold rush history continues as wine gold.

mythology.name: Rakaihautu
mythology.role: Ancestor who carved the South Island lakes
mythology.note: Māori legend holds that the great chief Rakaihautu carved Central Otago's lakes with his kō (digging tool) — creating the landscapes that would become the world's most southerly fine wine region.

appellations.name: Bannockburn Pinot Noir
appellations.description: Central Otago's finest sub-region. Schist-covered terraces above the Kawarau River gorge, severe continental climate and extraordinary UV intensity produce Pinot Noir of intense dark fruit, structure and mineral backbone — darker and more powerful than Marlborough, closer to a great Burgundy Premier Cru in character.
appellations.climate: Continental — extreme in New Zealand terms. Cold winters, hot dry summers, massive diurnal range.

tastingProfile.style: The world's southernmost Pinot — dark, intense, schist-mineral backbone

description: The indestructible wine. Madeira — oxidised, heated in estufas or canteiro, aged for decades — is effectively immortal. A bottle from 1795 is still alive. The island's volcanic soils and four noble grape varieties (Sercial, Verdelho, Boal, Malmsey) produce wines from bone-dry to lusciously sweet.

mythology.name: Zarco
mythology.note: João Gonçalves Zarco discovered Madeira in 1419 and planted the island's first vines. The island's name means 'wood' — explorers burned the dense forest, and the resulting ash created Madeira's extraordinarily fertile volcanic soils.

appellations.name: Madeira Vintage / Colheita
appellations.description: Madeira's unique estufagem (heating) or canteiro (gradual sun-warming) process, combined with deliberate oxidation and perpetual fortification, produces the world's only truly immortal wine. Vintage Madeiras from the 1800s are regularly drunk today. The four noble varieties offer a complete spectrum: Sercial (bone-dry, nervy), Verdelho (off-dry, smoky), Boal (medium-sweet, nutty), Malmsey (rich, caramelised).
appellations.climate: Subtropical Atlantic — perpetually mild, volcanic soils, high humidity.

tastingProfile.style: The immortal wine — oxidised, complex, 200 years of life

description: New York's most exciting wine region — Riesling country in an unlikely place. The deep glacial Finger Lakes moderate the continental climate, enabling Riesling to achieve Mosel-level complexity. Dr. Konstantin Frank's arrival in 1962 proved vinifera could thrive here.

mythology.name: Haudenosaunee Great Spirit
mythology.note: The Haudenosaunee Confederacy's creation story holds that the Great Spirit pressed His hands into the earth to bless it, leaving the elongated Finger Lakes. The lakes' depth (over 180m) and thermal mass make fine wine possible in upstate New York.

appellations.name: Seneca Lake Riesling
appellations.description: The deepest of the Finger Lakes at 187m — Seneca Lake never freezes, moderating the brutal upstate New York winters. Riesling here shows Alsatian texture combined with Germanic precision — Dr. Konstantin Frank's 1962 plantings proved that the Finger Lakes could produce world-class vinifera. Hermann J. Wiemer's Rieslings are internationally respected.
appellations.climate: Continental modified by Seneca Lake's thermal mass — lake-effect warming allows vinifera to survive harsh winters.

tastingProfile.style: America's Germanic Riesling — lake-cooled, slate-mineral, genuinely great
